intro.description: Something serious just happened — a kid in danger, an adult preying on a member, a disclosure of abuse, a real threat. The goal isn’t to handle it perfectly alone. It’s to act early enough, clearly enough, and with the right people.

moderator.banner: Moderators: you don’t handle these. You’re a spotter, not the responder — escalate to @Facilitators immediately with a message link, and never screenshot imagery of a minor.

closeout.title: Always close out
steps: Complete the Incident Report — what happened (dates, times, message links), who was involved, what you did and who you looped in, what was reported and when, and what follow-up is owed and who owns it.
Log it in #flags-log so the Facilitator team has the picture — keep sensitive detail appropriate to the channel.
Debrief and check on whoever responded. This work carries real weight; don’t let the responder just move on alone.
If the concern involves a staff member, run the Reporting Hierarchy tool too.

q1.text: Is anyone in immediate physical danger right now?

end-911.headline: Call 911 / relevant emergency services now.
steps: Discord can’t dispatch help — this is why we collect city/province and an emergency contact.
Use the location and emergency contact on file to direct responders.
Once everyone is safe: if this involved abuse, neglect, grooming, or exploitation, run this tool again and pick the incident type — some of these carry a legal duty to report.

s1.headline: First, together
steps: Don’t go it alone. Loop in the Manager of Youth Programs — or the COO if unavailable — right away. Flag it in #flags-log.
Contain it. Take sensitive conversations private (DM); restrict the offending account (mute or @Inactive role) if someone’s actively causing harm.

Preserve information — carefully. Note message links, usernames, timestamps, what happened, who’s involved. Hard exception: suspected sexual imagery of a minor — do NOT download, save, screenshot, or forward it. Possessing or sharing it can be a crime and re-victimizes the child. Note context only; Discord preserves the evidence on their end.
Stay in your lane. You’re not the investigator, therapist, or police. Your job: keep them safe, document, escalate, refer.
s1.roleNote: Moderators: your version of this list is one line — ping @Facilitators with the message link, now.

q2.text: What kind of incident is it?

hint.label: How do I recognize grooming?
hint.body: Grooming rarely looks alarming in any single interaction — it’s a pattern. Watch for an adult (or older teen) giving a youth special attention or flattery, gifts (Nitro, skins, gift cards), secrecy (‘just between us’), moving chats to DMs or other platforms, slowly boundary-pushing or sexualized talk, pulling them away from friends and family, or asking for photos or personal information. No single sign proves anything; the pattern is the signal. If your gut says something’s off, bring it to the team early — that’s what we’re here for.

answers.label: Danger to self or others — self-harm, suicide, threats
Grooming — an adult building an inappropriate relationship with a youth
Sexual exploitation / sexual content involving a minor

g1.text: Does your concern involve a staff member?

g2.text: Is it happening online or in person?

end-g-staff.headline: Take it up the chain now — one level above whoever’s involved.
steps: Report it through the Reporting Hierarchy Tool immediately — to the level above whoever is involved.
This never waits and never gets handled quietly.

end-g-inperson.headline: Treat this as a protection concern — continue in the Confidentiality Tool.
steps: A youth being groomed in person is a youth in need of protection. You don’t need certainty, and investigating is not your job.
Continue in the Confidentiality Tool — it picks up at the right question: would contacting their emergency contact put them at more risk?

end-g-online.headline: Help them report it where it’s happening.
steps: Support the youth to report the person on the platform where it’s taking place — it likely needs to come from their own account. Don’t tip the person off.

Help them explain the situation to their trusted adult — unless contacting that adult would put them at more risk, in which case continue in the Confidentiality Tool, which picks up at exactly that question.

end-a.headline: Take it private, listen, and get help in place.
steps: Take it to a private space (DM).

Supportive listening: acknowledge feelings, don’t debate, don’t promise what you can’t deliver. If you’re ASIST-trained, draw on that training and its resources to guide the conversation toward safety.
Active emergency → 911, using the location and emergency contact on file.
Offer vetted crisis lines.
Stay with them until support is in place, within your capacity.
In a crisis you ACT — you don’t clinically assess risk. That’s outside our scope of practice.

moderator.headline: Danger to self or others — escalate, and stay kind.
steps: Ping @Facilitators immediately with the message link.
If a life is in danger, anyone calls 911 — you don’t wait.
You can share a crisis line and stay present in DM if the youth trusts you — supportive listening, no promises, no investigating.

end-b.category: Sexual exploitation / sexual content involving a minor
end-b.headline: Contain, report, and never save the imagery.
steps: Don’t engage or tip off the suspected predator.
Don’t delete the account if it would destroy the trail — restrict and contain instead.
Never save or screenshot imagery — note context only (who / where / when, links).
Report to Discord Trust & Safety (in-app), the child-exploitation tipline, and police.
Support the affected youth gently — they may not see themselves as a victim. Loop the team on who has the best rapport for follow-up.

moderator.headline: Grooming or exploitation — escalate now, touch nothing.
steps: Ping @Facilitators immediately with the message link.
Don’t engage the suspected predator, don’t warn them, don’t investigate.
Never save or screenshot imagery of a minor — note context only.

end-c.headline: Continue in the Confidentiality Tool.
steps: Continue in the Confidentiality Tool — it picks up at the right question: would contacting their emergency contact put them at more risk?

Our standard: a child is in need of intervention where there are reasonable and probable grounds to believe their survival, security, or development is endangered. Suspected abuse or neglect must be reported.
You report suspicions — no proof needed, you don’t investigate, and you can’t hand the duty to someone else. Unsure? Treat it as reportable and loop in the lead.
Report to the child-protection authority for where the youth lives.

moderator.headline: A disclosure of abuse or neglect — escalate now.
steps: Ping @Facilitators immediately with the message link.
Don’t promise the youth secrecy, and don’t investigate.
Stay with the youth in DM if you’re the one they trust.

end-d.headline: Contain the harm, then escalate.
steps: Contain: remove the offending content from public view — preserve evidence first — and restrict the account.
Credible threat → treat like danger to self or others and involve police.
Report the account to Discord.
Support anyone targeted.

moderator.headline: Doxxing, threats, or harassment — contain what you can, then escalate.
steps: Ping @Facilitators immediately with the message link.
If you can, note usernames and message links before anything disappears.
Check in on anyone targeted.

end-e.headline: Preserve context and escalate.
steps: Contain the situation and preserve context — message links, usernames, timestamps.
Escalate to the team.
Involve police and/or Discord as appropriate.

moderator.headline: Something illegal — escalate now.
steps: Ping @Facilitators immediately with the message link.
Note context — who, where, when, links — and don’t investigate further.
